text: a rare indication for laparoscopic splenectomy for trauma penetrating abdominal stab wound in left upper quadrant with splenic arterial blush a forty one year old male was brought to e r at two a m after being stabbed on the road in the left upper quadrant and posteriorly on the left back
He was hemodynamically stable.
Peritoneal signs were unreliable since the patient was drunk and under the effect of narcotics.
CT scan showed a gross arterial blush in the inferior pole of the spleen with associated free fluid.
Angioembolization in angiosuit was excluded given the penetrating abdominal trauma with proven peritoneal penetration.

and the consequent risk of hollow viscous and or diaphragmatic injuries laparoscopy was therefore decided for exploration and splenectomy splenic salvage was excluded given the large intra parenchymal arterial blush seen at ct scan supine position was preferred given the risk of a rapid conversion to laparotomy
in case of sudden hemodynamic instability or of unexpected and uncontrollable bleeding.
Trocar placement is performed using the open Hassan technique, with one trocar positioned at the umbilicus, two in the left flank, and a fourth in the epigastrium.
The surgeon carefully evacuates a minimal amount of the perisplenic blood clot to prevent the clot from dislodging during the procedure.
and eventually caused a sudden populous arterial bleeding.
The surgeon uses a suction tube to bluntly suspend the spleen, minimizing parenchymal trauma and preventing capsular tears that could lead to intraoperative bleeding.
The surgeon rapidly frees the spleen from its inferior attachments, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses a hook to free the spleen from its posterior and lateral attachments, specifically the splenocolic, splenophrenic, and splenorenal ligaments, to achieve sufficient mobilization and medial rotation of the organ.
It can be indicated in selected trauma patients.
The only true essential prerequisite for laparoscopy is hemodynamic stability when laparoscopy is performed by experienced hands.
The surgeon exposes the splenic hilum and controls it by clamping with a flexible endostapler, securing the area to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon completes the splenectomy by gradually stapling and dividing the splenic hilar vessel and short gastric vessels using a flexible endostapler, ensuring rapid completion of the procedure.
The surgeon staples and divides the splenic hilar vessels, followed by the stapling and division of the short gastric vessels using a separate load. The laparoscopic approach is utilized.

Other potential indications and diagnostic advantages of laparoscopy are the assessment of possible hollow viscous injuries in patients with not definite peritoneal signs or unreliable physical examination.
Bluntsplenic injuries with active contrast extravasation in stable patients are better treated with initial non-operative management and angioembolization.

however in patients with penetrating splenic injuries and signs of active bleeding diagnostic laparoscopy is reasonable upon hemodynamic stability with both purposes of ruling out potential associated injuries from stab wounds

such as diaphragmatic injuries and hollow viscous injuries, especially stomach, splenic colon flexure, and proximal jejunum if left upper quadrant penetrating trauma, and definitely manage a splenic lesion with active contrast extravasation with a laparoscopic splenectomy, therefore avoiding the potential drawbacks and failure of angioembolization in a patient with penetrating trauma.
The surgeon carefully explores the left diaphragm, splenic-colonic flexure, and gastric greater curvature, visually confirming the absence of associated injuries in these areas.
Following irrigation, the surgeon verifies the lavage fluid is clear and applies topical hemostatic agents to the splenic bed.
Corrugated drains are inserted into the left upper quadrant and Douglas pouch, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon places the entire spleen into a large endo bag and extracts the morcellated splenic parenchyma through the umbilical port, ensuring protected removal.

The best indication for laparoscopy in trauma is for diagnosis and eventual management of diaphragmatic tears after left upper quadrant penetrating trauma or of hollow viscous injuries for stab wounds penetrating abdominal trauma in stable patients.
Post-operative recovery was uneventful and the patient was discharged on the fourth post-operative day.
